<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野速玉祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野速玉祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野速玉祭]]
{{Image requested|祭礼の様子が分かる画像、特に神馬、神輿、御船}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising draft}}
'''The Kumano Hayatama Festival''' is the annual festival of {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} ( {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū|1=Shingū, Wakayama}} {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|lt=Wakayama|City, Wakayama|8=en}} Prefecture). It is usually held from October 15th to 16th and is known for '''the Shinme Togyoshiki Ceremony''' on the 15th and '''the Ship Festival''' on the 16th.
The ship festival is sometimes called {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|en:draft||ja|お船祭り}} or Mifune Matsuri
It has been designated as {{ill|10=ja|11=重要無形民俗文化財|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|lt=an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|8=en}} of Japan (designated on {{ill|Heisei period|March|8=en}} 2, {{ill|2016|en|2016|WD=Q25245|lt=2016|1=2016}}, together with {{ill|10=ja|11=御燈祭|Oto Festival|lt=the Oto Festival|8=en}} ) .
== Kumano Gongen Arrival Festival == Hayatama Taisha Shrine enshrines several deities collectively known as the "Kumano Jusansho Gongen," and this festival is dedicated to the main deities, {{ill|10=ja|11=熊野権現|Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Okami|fr|8=en}} and Fusumi-no-Okami. According to a religious folklore interpretation, the festival recreates the arrival of Kumano Gongen, a deity from the world of eternity that traveled up {{ill|Kumano River|en|Kumano River|WD=Q1972071|lt=the Kumano River|1=Kumano River}}, enshrined on {{ill|10=ja|11=御船島|Mifune Island|8=en}}, and then passed through Otomoto-gawara and moved to Shingu <ref>桜井[1988: 172]</ref> .
The sacred horse procession ceremony on October 15th ( [[#The sacred horse procession (October 15th)|see below]] )
# Welcoming the spirit of {{ill|10=ja|11=阿須賀神社|Asuka Shrine|8=en}} to the second hall of Hayatama Taisha Shrine # The divine spirit travels to the shrine # After the ritual at the shrine, the divine spirit returns to the second shrine.
The structure is as follows. However, this structure cannot be seen as simply a transfer of the shrine from Asuka Shrine to Hayatama Taisha Shrine. <ref>桜井[1988: 167]</ref> According to the legends of Kumano Gongen found in "The Origin of the Kumano Gongen Descendants" (collected in {{ill|10=ja|11=長寛勘文|the Chokan Kanbun|8=en}} ), etc., Kumano Gongen first descended to {{ill|10=ja|11=神倉神社|12=fr|13=Kamikura-jinja|Kamikura Shrine|8=en}} in the Kumano region, and was then {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=enshrined|1=Bunrei}} in Iwabuchi Valley <ref> 今日の{{ill|貴禰谷神社|8=en}}（{{ill|三重県|8=en}}{{ill|紀宝町|8=en}}）と考えられている[平凡社 1997: 221]。</ref> to the north of Asuka Shrine, where he was first called Yuihayatama-Iezumimiko. It is said that the shrine was later moved from Ishibuchidani to Shingu <ref>桜井[1988: 166]</ref>, not from Asuka to Shingu. In other words, the structure of the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony represents the process by which Kumano Gongen was transferred from Ishibuchi-dani to Shingu. <ref>桜井[1988: 168]</ref>
Furthermore, until the Meiji era, the festival was held on September 15th and 16th of the lunar calendar, which is the last day of autumn and, according to early modern records, {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=dengaku|1=Dengaku}} was also held on the 14th. <ref>桜井[1998: 173]</ref> For these reasons, the Hayatama Festival has the characteristics of an autumn harvest festival. <ref>桜井[1998: 184]</ref>
== Depends on the festival == The festival begins on the previous day, October 14th. On this day, the sacred horse is taken to Ohama Beach, where it is purified in the sea. After that, two ceremonies are held: the "Mamekenjinogi" ceremony, in which beans are eaten at the former auxiliary shrine, Asuka Shrine, and the "Kakeuokayahokennogi" ceremony, in which offerings are received from the Oshima district {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=of Kushimoto Town|1=Kushimoto, Wakayama}} . <ref>上野[1998: 20]</ref> Of the offerings donated from the Oshima district, the hanging fish will be used at the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony on the 15th, and the Kayaho will be used at the Mifune Festival on the 16th. <ref>上野[1998: 21-22]</ref>
=== The sacred horse procession (October 15th) === A festival for Hayatama-no-Okami (enshrined in the second shrine). Starting in the afternoon of October 15th, the chief priest and his entourage head to Asuka Shrine in a procession, place a saddle on the sacred horse inside the shrine hall, and then welcome the deity. When the sacred horse carrying the divine spirit returns to the shrine, the divine spirit is transferred to the second shrine building, where prayers and kagura dance are recited and a Shinko festival is held. The chief priest then welcomes the divine spirit back onto the sacred horse, and accompanied by a procession of up to 200 people, including guards, musicians, Kagura performers, priests, and attendants, he travels along the banks of the Kumano River to Suginokarimiya (Otabisho), located northwest of {{ill|10=ja|11=千穂ヶ峯|Mount Chihogamine |lld|Mont Chihogamine |8=en}} . The chief priest then transfers the divine spirit to a temporary shrine, recites kagura and prayers in the light of torches, and makes offerings of fish, sacred sake, and food. After the ceremony is over, he returns to the main shrine in the darkness of night, carrying the divine spirit. <ref>以上、上野[1998: 21]</ref>
=== Ship Festival (October 16) === [[File:御船祭.JPG|thumb|280x280px| As the sun sets, a fast boat returns to the riverbank behind Hayatama Taisha Shrine (2014).]] [[File:御船祭_早船.JPG|thumb|280x280px| Even after the fast boat race is over, they continue to row their boat bravely (2014).]] A festival held for Fusumi-no-Okami (enshrined in the first hall). Starting at 8:00 a.m. on October 16th, the day after the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony, festival workers begin preparing the portable shrine, sacred boat, and other ritual implements. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210">豊島[1992: 210]</ref> Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine has in its possession a portable shrine that was donated by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} and has been designated an Important Cultural Property, but the one used in the shrine's festivals is a replacement. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_169">桜井[1988: 169]</ref> Once preparations are complete, the mikoshi is placed in front of the first shrine. The ship is entirely painted vermilion and has a black lacquered Chinese-style throne hall, and is a designated Important Cultural Property. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_169" /> Hitotsumono is a doll placed on a sacred horse. It is dressed in a hunting costume of gold brocade, has 12 reeds and 12 {{ill|10=ja|11=熊野牛王符|Kumano Goōfu|8=en}} attached to its waist, and wears a straw hat. It is interpreted as a representation of the spirit of Kumano Gongen. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210" /> <ref name="Sakurai_1988_170">桜井[1988: 170]</ref>
In the afternoon, a ceremony is held at the first shrine, and the chief priest transfers the divine spirit to a portable shrine. Following the Imperial flag, the Hitotsumono leads the festival procession to the banks of the Kumano River. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_170">桜井[1988: 170]</ref> The masked chief priest transfers the divine spirit from the portable shrine to the sacred boat, and the chief priest, other priests, and musicians board the main boat. The sacred boat and the main boat are towed by Morotabune boats, which are in turn led by nine fast boats. The person steering the Morote-bune is a resident of Udono (Kiho Town, Mie Prefecture) at the mouth of the Kumano River. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212">豊島[1992: 212]</ref> This is based on a legend that when Kumano Gongen was being moved from Ishibuchi-dani to Shingu, the residents of Udono led the way in boats. <ref>平凡社[1983]</ref> Additionally, on the Morote-bune boats, men dressed as women and clad in red robes, known as Hariwaise, stand on the side of the boats holding oars. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212" /> <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref>
The fast boat race begins after passing the rapids called Ushinohana, one kilometer downstream from Mifune Island. The fast boats compete for victory by making three clockwise laps around Mifune Island and then dock at Otomo Riverbed. Afterwards, the Morote-bune and the other three boats slowly circle Mifune Island twice. At this time, the Hariwaise performs the "Harihari dance" in which he looks into the distance with one hand raised while chanting "Hariharise" several times. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212">豊島[1992: 212]</ref> <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref> Next, an envoy from Mifunejima waves a fan three times, and the nine fast boats begin the race again. This time, we go around Mifunejima Island twice from the left and head towards the riverbank behind the shrine.
On the other hand, the sacred boat and the Saishu boat land at Otogigawara. The procession of priests then travels to the shrine together with the divine spirit that has been transferred to the mikoshi, and after carrying out the same ritual as the previous night, they return to the main shrine, again in the darkness of night, carrying the divine spirit, where it is placed in the second shrine <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref> <ref>豊島[1992:212-213]</ref> and the festival ends.
== Cultural Assets ==
* Hayatama Festival and Oto Festival in Shingu - Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan (designated on March 2, 2016, together with {{ill|10=ja|11=御燈祭|Oto Festival|lt=the Oto Festival|8=en}} ) <ref name="国指定">{{国指定文化財等データベース2|name=新宮の速玉祭・御燈祭り|register=302|item=00000934|year=2016|jyear=平成28|month=3|date=2|cat1=重要無形民俗文化財|class=風俗慣習、祭礼（信仰）|other=|accessdate=2016-07-31}}、平成28年3月2日文部科学省告示第40号</ref> ** It was previously designated as {{ill|10=ja|11=民俗文化財|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|lt=an Intangible Folk Cultural Property|8=en}} of Wakayama Prefecture (designated on May 28, {{ill|1964|en|1964|WD=Q2652|lt=1964|1=1964}} ( {{ill|Shōwa period|Showa|8=en}} 39)) <ref name="Bunkazai">{{Cite web|title=県指定文化財・民俗文化財|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenminzoku.html|access-date=2009-09-23|publisher=和歌山県教育委員会|和書}}</ref>, but following the national designation, its designation as a prefectural cultural property was removed <ref>[https://b2b-ch.infomart.co.jp/news/detail.page;JSESSIONID_B2BCH=61b050ab700812bab2d13b7a977a?0&IMNEWS1=914519 和歌山県指定文化財の新規指定等について]</ref> . * Kyuushitsu lacquered and gilt bronze-decorated portable shrine ('kyu' is made up of 'kamigashira' and 'rest') - said to have been an offering made by Ashikaga Yoshimitsu. Muromachi Period. Owned by Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine. It is designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|as an Important Cultural Property|8=en}} of Japan (art and crafts, designated on December 28, {{ill|1897|en|1897|WD=Q7847|lt=1897|1=1897}} ( {{ill|Meiji period|Meiji|8=en}} 30)) <ref>{{Cite web|title=きゅう漆金銅 装神輿|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5425|access-date=2010-06-12|website=国指定文化財等 データベース|publisher=文化庁|和書}}</ref> . * Kyushitsu lacquered, gilt bronze-decorated ship for divine service (kyuu kondousou shinkouyoufune, the "kyu" is "kamigashira" with the "rest" part) - Edo period. Owned by Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine. It is designated as an Important Cultural Property of Japan (art and crafts, designated on December 28, 1897 ( {{ill|Meiji period|Meiji|8=en}} 30)) <ref>{{Cite web|title=きゅう漆金銅装神幸用船|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5426|access-date=2010-06-12|website=国指定文化財等 データベース|publisher=文化庁|和書}}</ref> .

== Related item ==
==Older version==
{{Image request|images showing the festival, especially sacred horses, mikoshi, and ships|date=September 2009|cat=Shingu City|cat2=October}} '''Kumano Hayatama Festival''' (熊野速玉祭, Kumano Hayatama Matsuri) is the {{ill|annual festival|ja|例大祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual festival|ja_comment=fixed the redirect|1=Reisai|2=ja|3=例祭|4=fr|5=Reisai|lt=Reitaisai}} of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingu City|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}. It takes place every year from October 15 to 16 and is best known for the '''Shinme Togyo Ceremony''' (神馬渡御式) on October 15 and the '''Ofune Festival''' (御船祭) on October 16.
The festival has been designated a {{ill|National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by the Japanese government (March 2, 2016, together with the {{ill|Oto Festival|ja|御燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Festival|qq=}})<ref name="NationalDesignation">{{National Database of Cultural Properties 2|name=Shingu's Hayatama and Oto Festivals|register=302|item=00000934|year=2016|jyear=平成28|month=3|date=2|cat1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|class=Customs and Rituals, Festivals (Faith)|accessdate=July 31, 2016}}</ref>.
== Festival of Kumano Gongen's Arrival == The Kumano Hayatama Taisha enshrines multiple deities collectively known as the "Twelve Deities of Kumano." However, the main festival focuses on the principal deities, {{ill|Kumano Hayatamano Okami|ja|熊野速玉男神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatamano Okami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}} and {{ill|Fusumi Okami|ja|夫須美大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusumi Okami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to religious and folklore studies, the festival reenacts the journey of divine spirits ascending the {{ill|Kumano River|en|Kumano River|WD=Q1972071|lt=Kumano River|1=Kumano River}}, settling on {{ill|Ofunejima Island|ja|御船島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofunejima Island|qq=}}, and then moving through Otsumoto River to the shrine in Shingu<ref>Sakurai [1988: 172]</ref>.
On October 15, the {{ill|Shinme Togyo Ceremony|ja|神馬渡御式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinme Togyo Ceremony|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (see [[#Shinme Togyo Ceremony (October 15)|below]]) involves: 1. Welcoming the deity from {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} to the second hall of Kumano Hayatama Taisha. 2. A procession of the deity to the {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|WD=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}} (temporary shrine). 3. Returning the deity to the second hall after rituals.
This structure does not merely represent a relocation from Asuka Shrine to Kumano Hayatama Taisha. According to Kumano legends, the deities first descended at {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|lt=Kamikura Shrine|lt=Kamizakura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizakura Shrine|qq=}}, were enshrined at a place north of Asuka Shrine, and then relocated to Shingu<ref>Sakurai [1988: 166]</ref>.
Historically, the festival occurred on the 15th and 16th days of the ninth lunar month, a time associated with autumn harvest celebrations<ref>Sakurai [1988: 184]</ref>.
== Festival Schedule == The festival begins on October 14 with purification rituals for the sacred horse at Ohama Beach. Additional ceremonies include: - **Mame Kenji no Gi**: The horse is offered beans at Asuka Shrine. - **Kakeuo Kaya-ho Houken no Gi**: Offerings from Oshima in {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto Town|1=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}<ref>Ueno [1998: 20]</ref>.
=== Shinme Togyo Ceremony (October 15) === This ceremony honors Kumano Hayatamano Okami (enshrined in the second hall). Starting in the afternoon, a procession brings the deity from Asuka Shrine to the second hall. The procession continues with a large entourage leading the deity to the Otabisho<ref>Ueno [1998: 21]</ref>.
=== Ofune Festival (October 16) === Dedicated to Fusumi Okami (enshrined in the first hall), this festival features a grand procession of mikoshi and ceremonial boats on the Kumano River<ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210">Toyoshima [1992: 210]</ref>. The event concludes with ceremonial rowing and rituals at Ofunejima Island<ref name="Ueno_1998_22">Ueno [1998: 22]</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == - **Shingu's Hayatama and Oto Festivals**: National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (2016)<ref name="NationalDesignation"/>. - **Gilded Portable Shrine**: Donated by Ashikaga Yoshimitsu, designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1897<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5425|title=Gilded Portable Shrine|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|accessdate=June 12, 2010}}</ref>. - **Gilded Sacred Boat**: Also a National Important Cultural Property (1897)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5426|title=Gilded Sacred Boat|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|accessdate=June 12, 2010}}</ref>.

"Festivals of the Three Mountains." In Wada, Atsushi (ed.), *Kumano Gongen*. Tokyo: Chikuma Shobo. * Toyoshima, Osamu. 1992. *Land of the Dead, Kumano - The Sacred Faith of the Japanese People.* Tokyo: Kodansha.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野速玉祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野速玉祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野速玉祭]]
{{Image requested|祭礼の様子が分かる画像、特に神馬、神輿、御船}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising draft}}
'''The Kumano Hayatama Festival''' is the annual festival of {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} ( {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|qid=Q823526|lt=Shingū|1=Shingū, Wakayama}} {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|lt=Wakayama|City, Wakayama|8=en}} Prefecture). It is usually held from October 15th to 16th and is known for '''the Shinme Togyoshiki Ceremony''' on the 15th and '''the Ship Festival''' on the 16th.
The ship festival is sometimes called {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|en:draft||ja|お船祭り}} or Mifune Matsuri
It has been designated as {{ill|10=ja|11=重要無形民俗文化財|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|lt=an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|8=en}} of Japan (designated on {{ill|Heisei period|March|8=en}} 2, {{ill|2016|en|2016|qid=Q25245|lt=2016|1=2016}}, together with {{ill|10=ja|11=御燈祭|Oto Festival|lt=the Oto Festival|8=en}} ) .
== Kumano Gongen Arrival Festival == Hayatama Taisha Shrine enshrines several deities collectively known as the "Kumano Jusansho Gongen," and this festival is dedicated to the main deities, {{ill|10=ja|11=熊野権現|Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Okami|fr|8=en}} and Fusumi-no-Okami. According to a religious folklore interpretation, the festival recreates the arrival of Kumano Gongen, a deity from the world of eternity that traveled up {{ill|Kumano River|en|Kumano River|qid=Q1972071|lt=the Kumano River|1=Kumano River}}, enshrined on {{ill|10=ja|11=御船島|Mifune Island|8=en}}, and then passed through Otomoto-gawara and moved to Shingu <ref>桜井[1988: 172]</ref> .
The sacred horse procession ceremony on October 15th ( [[#The sacred horse procession (October 15th)|see below]] )
# Welcoming the spirit of {{ill|10=ja|11=阿須賀神社|Asuka Shrine|8=en}} to the second hall of Hayatama Taisha Shrine # The divine spirit travels to the shrine # After the ritual at the shrine, the divine spirit returns to the second shrine.
The structure is as follows. However, this structure cannot be seen as simply a transfer of the shrine from Asuka Shrine to Hayatama Taisha Shrine. <ref>桜井[1988: 167]</ref> According to the legends of Kumano Gongen found in "The Origin of the Kumano Gongen Descendants" (collected in {{ill|10=ja|11=長寛勘文|the Chokan Kanbun|8=en}} ), etc., Kumano Gongen first descended to {{ill|10=ja|11=神倉神社|12=fr|13=Kamikura-jinja|Kamikura Shrine|8=en}} in the Kumano region, and was then {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|qid=Q195793|lt=enshrined|1=Bunrei}} in Iwabuchi Valley <ref> 今日の{{ill|貴禰谷神社|8=en}}（{{ill|三重県|8=en}}{{ill|紀宝町|8=en}}）と考えられている[平凡社 1997: 221]。</ref> to the north of Asuka Shrine, where he was first called Yuihayatama-Iezumimiko. It is said that the shrine was later moved from Ishibuchidani to Shingu <ref>桜井[1988: 166]</ref>, not from Asuka to Shingu. In other words, the structure of the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony represents the process by which Kumano Gongen was transferred from Ishibuchi-dani to Shingu. <ref>桜井[1988: 168]</ref>
Furthermore, until the Meiji era, the festival was held on September 15th and 16th of the lunar calendar, which is the last day of autumn and, according to early modern records, {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|qid=Q3022455|lt=dengaku|1=Dengaku}} was also held on the 14th. <ref>桜井[1998: 173]</ref> For these reasons, the Hayatama Festival has the characteristics of an autumn harvest festival. <ref>桜井[1998: 184]</ref>
== Depends on the festival == The festival begins on the previous day, October 14th. On this day, the sacred horse is taken to Ohama Beach, where it is purified in the sea. After that, two ceremonies are held: the "Mamekenjinogi" ceremony, in which beans are eaten at the former auxiliary shrine, Asuka Shrine, and the "Kakeuokayahokennogi" ceremony, in which offerings are received from the Oshima district {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|qid=Q1343733|lt=of Kushimoto Town|1=Kushimoto, Wakayama}} . <ref>上野[1998: 20]</ref> Of the offerings donated from the Oshima district, the hanging fish will be used at the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony on the 15th, and the Kayaho will be used at the Mifune Festival on the 16th. <ref>上野[1998: 21-22]</ref>
=== The sacred horse procession (October 15th) === A festival for Hayatama-no-Okami (enshrined in the second shrine). Starting in the afternoon of October 15th, the chief priest and his entourage head to Asuka Shrine in a procession, place a saddle on the sacred horse inside the shrine hall, and then welcome the deity. When the sacred horse carrying the divine spirit returns to the shrine, the divine spirit is transferred to the second shrine building, where prayers and kagura dance are recited and a Shinko festival is held. The chief priest then welcomes the divine spirit back onto the sacred horse, and accompanied by a procession of up to 200 people, including guards, musicians, Kagura performers, priests, and attendants, he travels along the banks of the Kumano River to Suginokarimiya (Otabisho), located northwest of {{ill|10=ja|11=千穂ヶ峯|Mount Chihogamine |lld|Mont Chihogamine |8=en}} . The chief priest then transfers the divine spirit to a temporary shrine, recites kagura and prayers in the light of torches, and makes offerings of fish, sacred sake, and food. After the ceremony is over, he returns to the main shrine in the darkness of night, carrying the divine spirit. <ref>以上、上野[1998: 21]</ref>
=== Ship Festival (October 16) === [[File:御船祭.JPG|thumb|280x280px| As the sun sets, a fast boat returns to the riverbank behind Hayatama Taisha Shrine (2014).]] [[File:御船祭_早船.JPG|thumb|280x280px| Even after the fast boat race is over, they continue to row their boat bravely (2014).]] A festival held for Fusumi-no-Okami (enshrined in the first hall). Starting at 8:00 a.m. on October 16th, the day after the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony, festival workers begin preparing the portable shrine, sacred boat, and other ritual implements. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210">豊島[1992: 210]</ref> Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine has in its possession a portable shrine that was donated by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|qid=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} and has been designated an Important Cultural Property, but the one used in the shrine's festivals is a replacement. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_169">桜井[1988: 169]</ref> Once preparations are complete, the mikoshi is placed in front of the first shrine. The ship is entirely painted vermilion and has a black lacquered Chinese-style throne hall, and is a designated Important Cultural Property. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_169" /> Hitotsumono is a doll placed on a sacred horse. It is dressed in a hunting costume of gold brocade, has 12 reeds and 12 {{ill|10=ja|11=熊野牛王符|Kumano Goōfu|8=en}} attached to its waist, and wears a straw hat. It is interpreted as a representation of the spirit of Kumano Gongen. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210" /> <ref name="Sakurai_1988_170">桜井[1988: 170]</ref>
In the afternoon, a ceremony is held at the first shrine, and the chief priest transfers the divine spirit to a portable shrine. Following the Imperial flag, the Hitotsumono leads the festival procession to the banks of the Kumano River. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_170">桜井[1988: 170]</ref> The masked chief priest transfers the divine spirit from the portable shrine to the sacred boat, and the chief priest, other priests, and musicians board the main boat. The sacred boat and the main boat are towed by Morotabune boats, which are in turn led by nine fast boats. The person steering the Morote-bune is a resident of Udono (Kiho Town, Mie Prefecture) at the mouth of the Kumano River. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212">豊島[1992: 212]</ref> This is based on a legend that when Kumano Gongen was being moved from Ishibuchi-dani to Shingu, the residents of Udono led the way in boats. <ref>平凡社[1983]</ref> Additionally, on the Morote-bune boats, men dressed as women and clad in red robes, known as Hariwaise, stand on the side of the boats holding oars. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212" /> <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref>
The fast boat race begins after passing the rapids called Ushinohana, one kilometer downstream from Mifune Island. The fast boats compete for victory by making three clockwise laps around Mifune Island and then dock at Otomo Riverbed. Afterwards, the Morote-bune and the other three boats slowly circle Mifune Island twice. At this time, the Hariwaise performs the "Harihari dance" in which he looks into the distance with one hand raised while chanting "Hariharise" several times. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212">豊島[1992: 212]</ref> <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref> Next, an envoy from Mifunejima waves a fan three times, and the nine fast boats begin the race again. This time, we go around Mifunejima Island twice from the left and head towards the riverbank behind the shrine.
On the other hand, the sacred boat and the Saishu boat land at Otogigawara. The procession of priests then travels to the shrine together with the divine spirit that has been transferred to the mikoshi, and after carrying out the same ritual as the previous night, they return to the main shrine, again in the darkness of night, carrying the divine spirit, where it is placed in the second shrine <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref> <ref>豊島[1992:212-213]</ref> and the festival ends.
== Cultural Assets ==
* Hayatama Festival and Oto Festival in Shingu - Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan (designated on March 2, 2016, together with {{ill|10=ja|11=御燈祭|Oto Festival|lt=the Oto Festival|8=en}} ) <ref name="国指定">{{国指定文化財等データベース2|name=新宮の速玉祭・御燈祭り|register=302|item=00000934|year=2016|jyear=平成28|month=3|date=2|cat1=重要無形民俗文化財|class=風俗慣習、祭礼（信仰）|other=|accessdate=2016-07-31}}、平成28年3月2日文部科学省告示第40号</ref> ** It was previously designated as {{ill|10=ja|11=民俗文化財|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|lt=an Intangible Folk Cultural Property|8=en}} of Wakayama Prefecture (designated on May 28, {{ill|1964|en|1964|qid=Q2652|lt=1964|1=1964}} ( {{ill|Shōwa period|Showa|8=en}} 39)) <ref name="Bunkazai">{{Cite web|title=県指定文化財・民俗文化財|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenminzoku.html|access-date=2009-09-23|publisher=和歌山県教育委員会|和書}}</ref>, but following the national designation, its designation as a prefectural cultural property was removed <ref>[https://b2b-ch.infomart.co.jp/news/detail.page;JSESSIONID_B2BCH=61b050ab700812bab2d13b7a977a?0&IMNEWS1=914519 和歌山県指定文化財の新規指定等について]</ref> . * Kyuushitsu lacquered and gilt bronze-decorated portable shrine ('kyu' is made up of 'kamigashira' and 'rest') - said to have been an offering made by Ashikaga Yoshimitsu. Muromachi Period. Owned by Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine. It is designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|as an Important Cultural Property|8=en}} of Japan (art and crafts, designated on December 28, {{ill|1897|en|1897|qid=Q7847|lt=1897|1=1897}} ( {{ill|Meiji period|Meiji|8=en}} 30)) <ref>{{Cite web|title=きゅう漆金銅 装神輿|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5425|access-date=2010-06-12|website=国指定文化財等 データベース|publisher=文化庁|和書}}</ref> . * Kyushitsu lacquered, gilt bronze-decorated ship for divine service (kyuu kondousou shinkouyoufune, the "kyu" is "kamigashira" with the "rest" part) - Edo period. Owned by Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine. It is designated as an Important Cultural Property of Japan (art and crafts, designated on December 28, 1897 ( {{ill|Meiji period|Meiji|8=en}} 30)) <ref>{{Cite web|title=きゅう漆金銅装神幸用船|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5426|access-date=2010-06-12|website=国指定文化財等 データベース|publisher=文化庁|和書}}</ref> .

== Literature ==
* Gen Ueno, 1998, "Shinba-togoshiki and Obune-sai" in Takahisa Kato (ed.), Encyclopedia of the Faith in the Three Kumano Mountains, Ebisu Kosho Shuppan, ISBN 4900901075 * Mitsuru Sakurai, 1988, "Festivals of the Three Mountains", edited by Susumu Wada, "Kumano Gongen", Chikuma Shobo ISBN 4480854215 * All Japan Folk Performing Arts Association, 2006, Encyclopedia of Japanese Festival Culture, Tokyo Shoseki ISBN 4487733332 * Toyoshima, Osamu, 1992, "Kumano, the Land of Death - Japanese Sacred Land Beliefs", Kodansha (Kodansha Gendai Shinsho) ISBN 4061491032 * Heibonsha, 1983, "Place names of Mie Prefecture" (Japanese Historical Place Names Series 24), Heibonsha ISBN 9784582490244 * —, 1997, Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii, Heibonsha, ISBN 4582134025
== Related item ==
==Older version==
{{Image request|images showing the festival, especially sacred horses, mikoshi, and ships|date=September 2009|cat=Shingu City|cat2=October}} '''Kumano Hayatama Festival''' (熊野速玉祭, Kumano Hayatama Matsuri) is the {{ill|annual festival|ja|例大祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual festival|ja_comment=fixed the redirect|1=Reisai|2=ja|3=例祭|4=fr|5=Reisai|lt=Reitaisai}} of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|qid=Q823526|lt=Shingu City|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}. It takes place every year from October 15 to 16 and is best known for the '''Shinme Togyo Ceremony''' (神馬渡御式) on October 15 and the '''Ofune Festival''' (御船祭) on October 16.
The festival has been designated a {{ill|National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by the Japanese government (March 2, 2016, together with the {{ill|Oto Festival|ja|御燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Festival|qq=}})<ref name="NationalDesignation">{{National Database of Cultural Properties 2|name=Shingu's Hayatama and Oto Festivals|register=302|item=00000934|year=2016|jyear=平成28|month=3|date=2|cat1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|class=Customs and Rituals, Festivals (Faith)|accessdate=July 31, 2016}}</ref>.
== Festival of Kumano Gongen's Arrival == The Kumano Hayatama Taisha enshrines multiple deities collectively known as the "Twelve Deities of Kumano." However, the main festival focuses on the principal deities, {{ill|Kumano Hayatamano Okami|ja|熊野速玉男神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatamano Okami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}} and {{ill|Fusumi Okami|ja|夫須美大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusumi Okami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to religious and folklore studies, the festival reenacts the journey of divine spirits ascending the {{ill|Kumano River|en|Kumano River|qid=Q1972071|lt=Kumano River|1=Kumano River}}, settling on {{ill|Ofunejima Island|ja|御船島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofunejima Island|qq=}}, and then moving through Otsumoto River to the shrine in Shingu<ref>Sakurai [1988: 172]</ref>.
On October 15, the {{ill|Shinme Togyo Ceremony|ja|神馬渡御式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinme Togyo Ceremony|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (see [[#Shinme Togyo Ceremony (October 15)|below]]) involves: 1. Welcoming the deity from {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} to the second hall of Kumano Hayatama Taisha. 2. A procession of the deity to the {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|qid=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}} (temporary shrine). 3. Returning the deity to the second hall after rituals.
This structure does not merely represent a relocation from Asuka Shrine to Kumano Hayatama Taisha. According to Kumano legends, the deities first descended at {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|lt=Kamikura Shrine|lt=Kamizakura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizakura Shrine|qq=}}, were enshrined at a place north of Asuka Shrine, and then relocated to Shingu<ref>Sakurai [1988: 166]</ref>.
Historically, the festival occurred on the 15th and 16th days of the ninth lunar month, a time associated with autumn harvest celebrations<ref>Sakurai [1988: 184]</ref>.
== Festival Schedule == The festival begins on October 14 with purification rituals for the sacred horse at Ohama Beach. Additional ceremonies include: - **Mame Kenji no Gi**: The horse is offered beans at Asuka Shrine. - **Kakeuo Kaya-ho Houken no Gi**: Offerings from Oshima in {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|qid=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto Town|1=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}<ref>Ueno [1998: 20]</ref>.
=== Shinme Togyo Ceremony (October 15) === This ceremony honors Kumano Hayatamano Okami (enshrined in the second hall). Starting in the afternoon, a procession brings the deity from Asuka Shrine to the second hall. The procession continues with a large entourage leading the deity to the Otabisho<ref>Ueno [1998: 21]</ref>.
=== Ofune Festival (October 16) === Dedicated to Fusumi Okami (enshrined in the first hall), this festival features a grand procession of mikoshi and ceremonial boats on the Kumano River<ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210">Toyoshima [1992: 210]</ref>. The event concludes with ceremonial rowing and rituals at Ofunejima Island<ref name="Ueno_1998_22">Ueno [1998: 22]</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == - **Shingu's Hayatama and Oto Festivals**: National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (2016)<ref name="NationalDesignation"/>. - **Gilded Portable Shrine**: Donated by Ashikaga Yoshimitsu, designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1897<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5425|title=Gilded Portable Shrine|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|accessdate=June 12, 2010}}</ref>. - **Gilded Sacred Boat**: Also a National Important Cultural Property (1897)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5426|title=Gilded Sacred Boat|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|accessdate=June 12, 2010}}</ref>.
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}
== References == * Ueno, Motoharu. 1998. "Shinme Togyo Ceremony and Ofune Festival." In Kato, Takahisa (ed.), *Kumano Sanzan Faith Encyclopedia*. Tokyo: Ebisukosyo Shuppan. * Sakurai, Mitsuru. 1988. "Festivals of the Three Mountains." In Wada, Atsushi (ed.), *Kumano Gongen*. Tokyo: Chikuma Shobo. * Toyoshima, Osamu. 1992. *Land of the Dead, Kumano - The Sacred Faith of the Japanese People.* Tokyo: Kodansha.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Oto Festival|ja|御燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Festival|qq=}}: The grand fire festival held at the subsidiary {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|lt=Kamikura Shrine|lt=Kamizakura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizakura Shrine|qq=}}.

revision.text:
{{Image requested|祭礼の様子が分かる画像、特に神馬、神輿、御船}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising draft}}
'''The Kumano Hayatama Festival''' is the annual festival of {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} ( {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|qid=Q823526|lt=Shingū|1=Shingū, Wakayama}} {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|lt=Wakayama|City, Wakayama|8=en}} Prefecture). It is usually held from October 15th to 16th and is known for '''the Shinme Togyoshiki Ceremony''' on the 15th and '''the Ship Festival''' on the 16th.
The ship festival is sometimes called {{ill|Ofune Matsuri|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|en:draft||ja|お船祭り}} or Mifune Matsuri
It has been designated as {{ill|10=ja|11=重要無形民俗文化財|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|lt=an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|8=en}} of Japan (designated on {{ill|Heisei period|March|8=en}} 2, {{ill|2016|en|2016|qid=Q25245|lt=2016|1=2016}}, together with {{ill|10=ja|11=御燈祭|Oto Festival|lt=the Oto Festival|8=en}} ) .
== Kumano Gongen Arrival Festival == Hayatama Taisha Shrine enshrines several deities collectively known as the "Kumano Jusansho Gongen," and this festival is dedicated to the main deities, {{ill|10=ja|11=熊野権現|Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Okami|fr|8=en}} and Fusumi-no-Okami. According to a religious folklore interpretation, the festival recreates the arrival of Kumano Gongen, a deity from the world of eternity that traveled up {{ill|Kumano River|en|Kumano River|qid=Q1972071|lt=the Kumano River|1=Kumano River}}, enshrined on {{ill|10=ja|11=御船島|Mifune Island|8=en}}, and then passed through Otomoto-gawara and moved to Shingu <ref>桜井[1988: 172]</ref> .
The sacred horse procession ceremony on October 15th ( [[#The sacred horse procession (October 15th)|see below]] )
# Welcoming the spirit of {{ill|10=ja|11=阿須賀神社|Asuka Shrine|8=en}} to the second hall of Hayatama Taisha Shrine # The divine spirit travels to the shrine # After the ritual at the shrine, the divine spirit returns to the second shrine.
The structure is as follows. However, this structure cannot be seen as simply a transfer of the shrine from Asuka Shrine to Hayatama Taisha Shrine. <ref>桜井[1988: 167]</ref> According to the legends of Kumano Gongen found in "The Origin of the Kumano Gongen Descendants" (collected in {{ill|10=ja|11=長寛勘文|the Chokan Kanbun|8=en}} ), etc., Kumano Gongen first descended to {{ill|10=ja|11=神倉神社|12=fr|13=Kamikura-jinja|Kamikura Shrine|8=en}} in the Kumano region, and was then {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|qid=Q195793|lt=enshrined|1=Bunrei}} in Iwabuchi Valley <ref> 今日の{{ill|貴禰谷神社|8=en}}（{{ill|三重県|8=en}}{{ill|紀宝町|8=en}}）と考えられている[平凡社 1997: 221]。</ref> to the north of Asuka Shrine, where he was first called Yuihayatama-Iezumimiko. It is said that the shrine was later moved from Ishibuchidani to Shingu <ref>桜井[1988: 166]</ref>, not from Asuka to Shingu. In other words, the structure of the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony represents the process by which Kumano Gongen was transferred from Ishibuchi-dani to Shingu. <ref>桜井[1988: 168]</ref>
Furthermore, until the Meiji era, the festival was held on September 15th and 16th of the lunar calendar, which is the last day of autumn and, according to early modern records, {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|qid=Q3022455|lt=dengaku|1=Dengaku}} was also held on the 14th. <ref>桜井[1998: 173]</ref> For these reasons, the Hayatama Festival has the characteristics of an autumn harvest festival. <ref>桜井[1998: 184]</ref>
== Depends on the festival == The festival begins on the previous day, October 14th. On this day, the sacred horse is taken to Ohama Beach, where it is purified in the sea. After that, two ceremonies are held: the "Mamekenjinogi" ceremony, in which beans are eaten at the former auxiliary shrine, Asuka Shrine, and the "Kakeuokayahokennogi" ceremony, in which offerings are received from the Oshima district {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|qid=Q1343733|lt=of Kushimoto Town|1=Kushimoto, Wakayama}} . <ref>上野[1998: 20]</ref> Of the offerings donated from the Oshima district, the hanging fish will be used at the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony on the 15th, and the Kayaho will be used at the Mifune Festival on the 16th. <ref>上野[1998: 21-22]</ref>
=== The sacred horse procession (October 15th) === A festival for Hayatama-no-Okami (enshrined in the second shrine). Starting in the afternoon of October 15th, the chief priest and his entourage head to Asuka Shrine in a procession, place a saddle on the sacred horse inside the shrine hall, and then welcome the deity. When the sacred horse carrying the divine spirit returns to the shrine, the divine spirit is transferred to the second shrine building, where prayers and kagura dance are recited and a Shinko festival is held. The chief priest then welcomes the divine spirit back onto the sacred horse, and accompanied by a procession of up to 200 people, including guards, musicians, Kagura performers, priests, and attendants, he travels along the banks of the Kumano River to Suginokarimiya (Otabisho), located northwest of {{ill|10=ja|11=千穂ヶ峯|Mount Chihogamine |lld|Mont Chihogamine |8=en}} . The chief priest then transfers the divine spirit to a temporary shrine, recites kagura and prayers in the light of torches, and makes offerings of fish, sacred sake, and food. After the ceremony is over, he returns to the main shrine in the darkness of night, carrying the divine spirit. <ref>以上、上野[1998: 21]</ref>
=== Ship Festival (October 16) === [[File:御船祭.JPG|thumb|280x280px| As the sun sets, a fast boat returns to the riverbank behind Hayatama Taisha Shrine (2014).]] [[File:御船祭_早船.JPG|thumb|280x280px| Even after the fast boat race is over, they continue to row their boat bravely (2014).]] A festival held for Fusumi-no-Okami (enshrined in the first hall). Starting at 8:00 a.m. on October 16th, the day after the Shinba-togoshiki ceremony, festival workers begin preparing the portable shrine, sacred boat, and other ritual implements. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210">豊島[1992: 210]</ref> Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine has in its possession a portable shrine that was donated by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|qid=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} and has been designated an Important Cultural Property, but the one used in the shrine's festivals is a replacement. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_169">桜井[1988: 169]</ref> Once preparations are complete, the mikoshi is placed in front of the first shrine. The ship is entirely painted vermilion and has a black lacquered Chinese-style throne hall, and is a designated Important Cultural Property. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_169" /> Hitotsumono is a doll placed on a sacred horse. It is dressed in a hunting costume of gold brocade, has 12 reeds and 12 {{ill|10=ja|11=熊野牛王符|Kumano Goōfu|8=en}} attached to its waist, and wears a straw hat. It is interpreted as a representation of the spirit of Kumano Gongen. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210" /> <ref name="Sakurai_1988_170">桜井[1988: 170]</ref>
In the afternoon, a ceremony is held at the first shrine, and the chief priest transfers the divine spirit to a portable shrine. Following the Imperial flag, the Hitotsumono leads the festival procession to the banks of the Kumano River. <ref name="Sakurai_1988_170">桜井[1988: 170]</ref> The masked chief priest transfers the divine spirit from the portable shrine to the sacred boat, and the chief priest, other priests, and musicians board the main boat. The sacred boat and the main boat are towed by Morotabune boats, which are in turn led by nine fast boats. The person steering the Morote-bune is a resident of Udono (Kiho Town, Mie Prefecture) at the mouth of the Kumano River. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212">豊島[1992: 212]</ref> This is based on a legend that when Kumano Gongen was being moved from Ishibuchi-dani to Shingu, the residents of Udono led the way in boats. <ref>平凡社[1983]</ref> Additionally, on the Morote-bune boats, men dressed as women and clad in red robes, known as Hariwaise, stand on the side of the boats holding oars. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212" /> <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref>
The fast boat race begins after passing the rapids called Ushinohana, one kilometer downstream from Mifune Island. The fast boats compete for victory by making three clockwise laps around Mifune Island and then dock at Otomo Riverbed. Afterwards, the Morote-bune and the other three boats slowly circle Mifune Island twice. At this time, the Hariwaise performs the "Harihari dance" in which he looks into the distance with one hand raised while chanting "Hariharise" several times. <ref name="Toyoshima_1992_212">豊島[1992: 212]</ref> <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref> Next, an envoy from Mifunejima waves a fan three times, and the nine fast boats begin the race again. This time, we go around Mifunejima Island twice from the left and head towards the riverbank behind the shrine.
On the other hand, the sacred boat and the Saishu boat land at Otogigawara. The procession of priests then travels to the shrine together with the divine spirit that has been transferred to the mikoshi, and after carrying out the same ritual as the previous night, they return to the main shrine, again in the darkness of night, carrying the divine spirit, where it is placed in the second shrine <ref name="Ueno_1998_22">上野[1998: 22]</ref> <ref>豊島[1992:212-213]</ref> and the festival ends.
== Cultural Assets ==
* Hayatama Festival and Oto Festival in Shingu - Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan (designated on March 2, 2016, together with {{ill|10=ja|11=御燈祭|Oto Festival|lt=the Oto Festival|8=en}} ) <ref name="国指定">{{国指定文化財等データベース2|name=新宮の速玉祭・御燈祭り|register=302|item=00000934|year=2016|jyear=平成28|month=3|date=2|cat1=重要無形民俗文化財|class=風俗慣習、祭礼（信仰）|other=|accessdate=2016-07-31}}、平成28年3月2日文部科学省告示第40号</ref> ** It was previously designated as {{ill|10=ja|11=民俗文化財|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|lt=an Intangible Folk Cultural Property|8=en}} of Wakayama Prefecture (designated on May 28, {{ill|1964|en|1964|qid=Q2652|lt=1964|1=1964}} ( {{ill|Shōwa period|Showa|8=en}} 39)) <ref name="Bunkazai">{{Cite web|title=県指定文化財・民俗文化財|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenminzoku.html|access-date=2009-09-23|publisher=和歌山県教育委員会|和書}}</ref>, but following the national designation, its designation as a prefectural cultural property was removed <ref>[https://b2b-ch.infomart.co.jp/news/detail.page;JSESSIONID_B2BCH=61b050ab700812bab2d13b7a977a?0&IMNEWS1=914519 和歌山県指定文化財の新規指定等について]</ref> . * Kyuushitsu lacquered and gilt bronze-decorated portable shrine ('kyu' is made up of 'kamigashira' and 'rest') - said to have been an offering made by Ashikaga Yoshimitsu. Muromachi Period. Owned by Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine. It is designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|as an Important Cultural Property|8=en}} of Japan (art and crafts, designated on December 28, {{ill|1897|en|1897|qid=Q7847|lt=1897|1=1897}} ( {{ill|Meiji period|Meiji|8=en}} 30)) <ref>{{Cite web|title=きゅう漆金銅 装神輿|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5425|access-date=2010-06-12|website=国指定文化財等 データベース|publisher=文化庁|和書}}</ref> . * Kyushitsu lacquered, gilt bronze-decorated ship for divine service (kyuu kondousou shinkouyoufune, the "kyu" is "kamigashira" with the "rest" part) - Edo period. Owned by Kumano Hayatama Taisha Shrine. It is designated as an Important Cultural Property of Japan (art and crafts, designated on December 28, 1897 ( {{ill|Meiji period|Meiji|8=en}} 30)) <ref>{{Cite web|title=きゅう漆金銅装神幸用船|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5426|access-date=2010-06-12|website=国指定文化財等 データベース|publisher=文化庁|和書}}</ref> .

== Literature ==

== Related item ==
==Older version==
{{Image request|images showing the festival, especially sacred horses, mikoshi, and ships|date=September 2009|cat=Shingu City|cat2=October}} '''Kumano Hayatama Festival''' (熊野速玉祭, Kumano Hayatama Matsuri) is the {{ill|annual festival|ja|例大祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual festival|ja_comment=fixed the redirect|1=Reisai|2=ja|3=例祭|4=fr|5=Reisai|lt=Reitaisai}} of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|qid=Q823526|lt=Shingu City|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}. It takes place every year from October 15 to 16 and is best known for the '''Shinme Togyo Ceremony''' (神馬渡御式) on October 15 and the '''Ofune Festival''' (御船祭) on October 16.
The festival has been designated a {{ill|National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by the Japanese government (March 2, 2016, together with the {{ill|Oto Festival|ja|御燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Festival|qq=}})<ref name="NationalDesignation">{{National Database of Cultural Properties 2|name=Shingu's Hayatama and Oto Festivals|register=302|item=00000934|year=2016|jyear=平成28|month=3|date=2|cat1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|class=Customs and Rituals, Festivals (Faith)|accessdate=July 31, 2016}}</ref>.
== Festival of Kumano Gongen's Arrival == The Kumano Hayatama Taisha enshrines multiple deities collectively known as the "Twelve Deities of Kumano." However, the main festival focuses on the principal deities, {{ill|Kumano Hayatamano Okami|ja|熊野速玉男神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatamano Okami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}} and {{ill|Fusumi Okami|ja|夫須美大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusumi Okami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to religious and folklore studies, the festival reenacts the journey of divine spirits ascending the {{ill|Kumano River|en|Kumano River|qid=Q1972071|lt=Kumano River|1=Kumano River}}, settling on {{ill|Ofunejima Island|ja|御船島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofunejima Island|qq=}}, and then moving through Otsumoto River to the shrine in Shingu<ref>Sakurai [1988: 172]</ref>.
On October 15, the {{ill|Shinme Togyo Ceremony|ja|神馬渡御式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinme Togyo Ceremony|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (see [[#Shinme Togyo Ceremony (October 15)|below]]) involves: 1. Welcoming the deity from {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} to the second hall of Kumano Hayatama Taisha. 2. A procession of the deity to the {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|qid=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}} (temporary shrine). 3. Returning the deity to the second hall after rituals.
This structure does not merely represent a relocation from Asuka Shrine to Kumano Hayatama Taisha. According to Kumano legends, the deities first descended at {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|lt=Kamikura Shrine|lt=Kamizakura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizakura Shrine|qq=}}, were enshrined at a place north of Asuka Shrine, and then relocated to Shingu<ref>Sakurai [1988: 166]</ref>.
Historically, the festival occurred on the 15th and 16th days of the ninth lunar month, a time associated with autumn harvest celebrations<ref>Sakurai [1988: 184]</ref>.
== Festival Schedule == The festival begins on October 14 with purification rituals for the sacred horse at Ohama Beach. Additional ceremonies include: - **Mame Kenji no Gi**: The horse is offered beans at Asuka Shrine. - **Kakeuo Kaya-ho Houken no Gi**: Offerings from Oshima in {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|qid=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto Town|1=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}<ref>Ueno [1998: 20]</ref>.
=== Shinme Togyo Ceremony (October 15) === This ceremony honors Kumano Hayatamano Okami (enshrined in the second hall). Starting in the afternoon, a procession brings the deity from Asuka Shrine to the second hall. The procession continues with a large entourage leading the deity to the Otabisho<ref>Ueno [1998: 21]</ref>.
=== Ofune Festival (October 16) === Dedicated to Fusumi Okami (enshrined in the first hall), this festival features a grand procession of mikoshi and ceremonial boats on the Kumano River<ref name="Toyoshima_1992_210">Toyoshima [1992: 210]</ref>. The event concludes with ceremonial rowing and rituals at Ofunejima Island<ref name="Ueno_1998_22">Ueno [1998: 22]</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == - **Shingu's Hayatama and Oto Festivals**: National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (2016)<ref name="NationalDesignation"/>. - **Gilded Portable Shrine**: Donated by Ashikaga Yoshimitsu, designated as a National Important Cultural Property in 1897<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5425|title=Gilded Portable Shrine|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|accessdate=June 12, 2010}}</ref>. - **Gilded Sacred Boat**: Also a National Important Cultural Property (1897)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5426|title=Gilded Sacred Boat|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|accessdate=June 12, 2010}}</ref>.
